Oncology Guidelines Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (Oncology Guidelines®)
Neuroendocrine and 
Adrenal Tumors
Version 1.2026 — April 21, 2026
Oncology Guidelines recognizes the importance of clinical trials and encourages participation when applicable and available. 
Trials should be designed to maximize inclusiveness and broad representative enrollment.

The Oncology Guidelines® are a statement of evidence and consensus of the authors regarding their views of currently accepted approaches to 
treatment. Any clinician seeking to apply or consult the Oncology Guidelines is expected to use independent medical judgment in the context of individual 
clinical circumstances to determine any patient’s care or treatment. The Oncology Guidelines® (Oncology Guidelines®) makes no representations 
or warranties of any kind regarding their content, use or application and disclaims any responsibility for their application or use in any way. The Oncology Guidelines

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Continued
Global:
• Footnote revised: Principles of Pathology for the Diagnosis of Neuroendocrine Neoplasms (NE-E).
• Principles of Endoscopic Treatment of NeuroendocrineTumors (NE-C) link added where appropriate.
• Liquid biopsy changed to circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) testing.
• Hyperlinks to appropriate Principles pages added where indicated.
• Principles of Systemic Anti-Tumor Therapy table headers revised with addition of: (alphabetical order by category, order does not imply preference)
Clinical Presentations and Diagnosis
CP-1
• Neuroendocrine Tumors (NETs) of the Gastrointestinal Tract (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2), Lung, and Thymus, new bullet added: Multiple lung 
nodules or tumorlets and evidence of diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia (DIPNECH) (NET-13)
• New header added: Management of SSTR-Negative Disease of Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2 NETs (SSTRNEG-1)
• New footnote b added: Refer to the Oncology Guidelines Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A).
Neuroendocrine Tumors of the Gastrointestinal Tract (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2), Lung and Thymus
NET-1
• Following Hyper-gastrinemic/Type 1, First-Line Treatment, options revised:
Endoscopic resection of >1 cm gastric tumors treatment (NE-C) → EGD at 1 y and then every 1–3 y thereafter
Removed: Metastatic disease (NET-9)
• Following Hper-gastrinemic/Type 2:
New column added: 
 ◊Multiphasic abdomen CT or MRI (NE-B)
 ◊SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI (NE-B)
First-Line Treatment, option revised: Endoscopic resection of >1 cm gastric tumors treatment (NE-C) and multidisciplinary approach to treat the 
underlying gastrinoma (See gastinoma (PanNET-3) for identification and treatment of the gastrin-producing tumor)
• Following Normal gastrin/Type 3:
First bullet revised: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) to determine depth of invasion and assess for lymphadenopathy
First-Line Treatment, bottom option revised: . . .or If no evidence of regional lymphadenopathy on EUS or other imaging, consider endoscopic 
resection (NE-C) or surgical wedge resection (NE-D) → Surveillance (NET-8)
• Following evaluation, bottom pathway revised: Other PPI-induced → Endoscopic treatment (NE-C)
NET-1A
• Footnote b revised: May need multiple biopsies throughout the entire stomach. The minimum standard is to obtain at least two biopsies from the antrum 
(lesser and greater curvature), one biopsy from the incisura (angularis), and two biopsies from the gastric body (lesser and greater curvature). Dixon 
MF, et al. Am J Surg Pathol 1996;20:1161-1181.
NET-2
• Surveillance, top option revised: Endoscopic surveillance every 3–12 mo for the first y and then annually thereafter (NE-C)
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
NET-3
• Following Simple appendectomy, third pathway revised: . . .or Any tumor size with incomplete resection or positive lymph nodes/margins within the 
appendectomy specimen (if available for analysis) (NE-D)
• Footnote p, link revised: See Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer Appendiceal Neoplasms and Cancers.
• Footnote removed: Data are limited on survival benefit from right hemicolectomy.
NET-4
• Evaluation, As appropriate, bullet removed: Consider baseline 5-HIAA in asymptomatic patients with high burden of metastatic disease (NE-A)
• Footnote u, new bullet added: Resection to root of mesentery.
NET-5
• Surveillance:
Top option revised: No additional follow-up required Endoscopic surveillance (NE-C)
Following T1, surveillance revised:
 ◊Bullets removed:
– <1 cm: No follow-up required
– 1 to ≤2 cm: Endoscopy with rectal MRI or endorectal ultrasound at 6 and 12 mo, then as clinically indicated
 ◊New option added: Endoscopic surveillance (NE-C)
NET-6
• Evaluation revised to: Consider if clinically indicated 
NET-8
• 12 wk–12 mo post-resection: 
Bullets removed:
 ◊Multiphasic abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI for primary GI NETs (as clinically indicated for lung and thymic NETs) (NE-B) (Also for >1 y post-resection 
to 10y:)
 ◊Chest CT + CT or MRI abdomen with contrast for primary lung/thymic NETs (as clinically indicated for primary GI tumors)
New bullets added: (Also for >1 y post-resection to 10y:)
 ◊Primary GI NETs
– Multiphasic abdomen ± pelvis CT or MRI (NE-B)
– Chest CT ± contrast (as clinically indicated)
 ◊Primary lung/thymic NETs
– Chest CT + multiphasic CT or MRI abdomen with contrast
– Pelvis CT or MRI with contrast (as clinically indicated) (NE-B)
• >1 y post-resection to 10y:
Bullet removed: Chest/abdomen CT with contrast for primary lung/thymic NETs (as clinically indicated for primary GI tumors)
NET-9
• Evaluation, fourth bullet revised: Routine Bbiochemical evaluation as clinically indicated with 5-HIAA (for ileal, jejunal, and cecal NETs) in patients with 
symptoms and consider in asymptomatic patients with metastatic disease (NE-A)
NET-10
• Subsequent Therapy, Locoregional therapy options, first bullet revised: Consider liver-directed therapy for liver-predominant disease (NE-H)
NET-11
• New footnote added: For information regarding DPYD testing, see the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer.
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:
Continued

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Neuroendocrine Tumors of the Pancreas (Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2)
PanNET-2
• Treatment column following >1 to ≤2cm revised: Observation in select cases or Pancreatectomy or Enucleation in select cases (NE-D)
• Footnote i revised: As appropriate, central pancreatectomy or spleen-preserving surgery should can be considered.
PanNET-4
• Following Resectable:
Options removed:
 ◊Duodenum → Duodenotomy and intraoperative ultrasound; local resection/ enucleation of tumor(s) + periduodenal node dissection (NE-D)
 ◊Head → Pancreatoduodenectomy (NE-D)
 ◊Distal → Distal pancreatectomy + splenectomy + regional nodes (NE-D)
New option added: Primary located → Resection
• Footnote removed: Preoperative vaccination against encapsulated bacterium (ie, pneumococcus, haemophilus influenzae b, meningococcal group C), if 
considering surgery with possible splenectomy. See Principles of Surgical Management of Neuroendocrine Tumors (NE-D 2 of 3).
PanNET-6
• Footnote q added: In highly-selected cases where the lesion is resectable but the patient is not a surgical candidate, EUS-guided ablation can be 
considered at a center with expertise.
PanNET-8
• Footnote removed: Small (<2 cm), peripheral glucagonomas are rare; enucleation/local excision + peripancreatic lymph dissection may be considered.
PanNET-10
• Footnote removed: Small (<2 cm), peripheral VIPomas are rare; enucleation/local excision + peripancreatic lymph dissection may be considered.
Management of SSTR-Negative Disease of Well-Differentiated Grade 1/2 NETs
SSTRNEG-1 
• New SSTR-Negative Disease recommendations added.
Well-Differentiated, Grade 3 Neuroendocrine Tumors
WDG3-2
• Treatment following Unfavorable biology, options revised: . . .Resection with regional lymphadenectomy if feasible if appropriate after multidisciplinary 
review, or Neoadjuvant chemo systemic therapy on a case-by-case basis (eg, Ki-67 ≥55%).
• Footnotes added:
Footnote a: Principles of Pathology (NE-E).
Footnote h: There is no role for adjuvant therapy outside of a clinical trial. Consider enrollment in a clinical trial.
WDG3-4
• Treatment, new option added: Resection may play a role in select cases (NE-D).
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:
Continued

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Extrapulmonary Poorly Differentiated: Neuroendocrine Carcinoma/Large or Small Cell Carcinoma/Mixed Neuroendocrine-Non-Neuroendocrine Neoplasm
PDNEC-1
• Evaluation:
Recommended:
 ◊First bullet revised: Multiphasic chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (NE-B) or
 ◊New bullet added: Molecular profiling of tumor tissue.
As appropriate, bullet removed: Consider molecular profiling of tumor tissue.
• Treatment, second column on bottom revised: If progression: (NE-G): Chemotherapy, Immunotherapy, Targeted therapy.
• Footnote g revised: Tumor/somatic molecular profiling should be considered is recommended for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic 
disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. Consider specifically testing for potentially actionable somatic 
findings including, but not limited to: NTRK fusions, RET fusions, BRAF V600E mutations, microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H), mismatch repair 
deficiency (dMMR), and tumor mutational burdenhigh (TMB-H). Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, liquid biopsy ctDNA testing can be 
considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible.
• Footnote removed: Pembrolizumab can be considered for patients with MSI-H, dMMR, or advanced TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) tumors (as determined by an 
FDA-approved test) that have progressed following prior treatment and have no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
Pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma
PHEO-1
• Evaluation, Recommended, first bullet revised: Plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated total metanephrines and normetanephrines catecholamines (NE-
A 2 of 4).
PHEO-3
• Surveillance:
Resectable disease:
 ◊12 wk–12 mo post-resection, first bullet revised: H&P, blood pressure, and plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated total metanephrines and 
normetanephrines catecholamines (NE-A 2 of 4)
 ◊>1 y post-curative resection up to 10y:
– First bullet revised: H&P, blood pressure, and markers annually or sooner if patient is symptomatic (NE-A 2 of 4)
– Sub-bullets removed:
▪Years 1–3: every 6–12 mo
	▪Years 4+ up to 10 y: annually
 ◊Header removed: >10 y:
– Bullet removed: Consider surveillance as clinically indicated
Locally unresectable disease or Distant metastases, first bullet revised: H&P, blood pressure, and plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated total 
metanephrines and normetanephrines catecholamines (NE-A 2 of 4)
Adrenal Gland Tumors
AGT-1
• Following Morphologic evaluation, Adrenal protocol CT scan, first bullet, first sub-bullet revised: If Hounsfield unit (HU) < +10, no further washout 
imaging needed on this scan
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:
Continued

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
AGT-2
• Following Primary aldosteronism, top option revised: Not a surgical candidate or surgery not desired per patient
AGT-3
• Additional Evaluation, Adrenal protocol CT scan, first sub-bullet, first tertiary-bullet revised: If Hounsfield unit (HU) < +10, no further washout imaging 
needed on this scan
• First-Line Treatment, bottom option revised: Open Adrenalectomy for suspected malignancy (NE-D)
AGT-4
• First-Line Treatment, top option revised: Open Adrenalectomy for suspected carcinoma (NE-D)
AGT-5
• Treatment following Localized disease, second column, third bullet revised: Consider adjuvant Mitotane therapy (category 3)
• Follow-Up interval for both pathways revised: Every 12 wk–12 mo up to 5 10 y (after 5 10 y as clinically indicated)
Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia, Type 1
MEN1-1
• Page header revised: MEN1 Surveillance Screening
• Parathyroid:
Bullet revised: Calcium and albumin annually
Second column, new bullet added: Alkaline phosphatase
• PanNET, second bullet revised: Consider abdomen/pelvis MRI (preferred) or CT or MRI with contrast every 1–3 y
• Pituitary, first bullet revised: Pituitary or sella MRI with contrast of pituitary every 3–5 y
• New header added: Other neoplasia
New bullets added:
 ◊Angiomas
 ◊Adrenal tumors
 ◊Collagenomas
 ◊Lipomas
 ◊Angiofibromas
• New footnote c added: Further studies should be done, especially in patients with suspected MEN1, when the serum calcium is ≥10 mg/dL since 
normocalcemic hyperparathyroidism is noted especially as an early sign of the disease. Hyperparathyroidism evaluations should include consideration 
of other secondary causes of hypercalcemia.
MEN1-2
• Far left column, new bullet added: Other tumors may be associated with MEN
• Clinical Evaluation:
Parathyroid, first bullet, new sub-bullet added: Consider bone mineral density (if age appropriate)
Pituitary:
 ◊First sub-bullet revised: Pituitary or sella MRI with contrast
 ◊New sub-bullet added: Consider referral to endocrinology and neurosurgery for further workup
• Treatment, following Pituitary, option revised: Consider referral to endocrinology for further workup Medical versus surgical treatment based on hormone 
function and/or tumor size
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:
Continued

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
MEN1-2 (continued)
• New footnotes added:
Footnote e: Clinical presentation dictates the most appropriate biochemical and radiologic investigation
Footnote i: Studies showed better prognosis when thymectomy is done at same time as partial parathyroidectomy. 
Footnote j: Parathyroid surgery will likely differ for spontaneous hyperparathyroidism.
Footnote k: For surgical approach, recommend evaluation by high-volume parathyroid surgeon.
MEN1-3
• Bullet removed: Endoscopy with EUS is recommended prior to pancreatic surgery in order to preoperatively assess and localize tumors.
MEN1-4
• New references added: 
Reference 1: Brandi ML, et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2025;13:699-721. Published correction appears in Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 
2025;13:e14.
Reference 8: Toraih EA, AbdAlnaeem MA, Bobba T, et al. The role of thymectomy during parathyroidectomy in multiple endocrine neoplasia type 
1-associated hyperparathyroidism: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Surg Oncol 2025;23:272.
• References removed:
Yates CJ, Newey PJ, Thakker RV. Challenges and controversies in management of pancreatic neuroendocrine tumours in patients with MEN1. Lancet 
Diabetes Endocrinol 2015;3:895-905.
Frost M, Lines KE, Thakker RV. Current and emerging therapies for PNETs in patients with or without MEN1. Nat Rev Endocrinol 2018;14:216-227.
NE-A 1 of 4
• Carcinoid syndrome (NETs of GI tract), Location revised: Primary tumors in small bowel and appendix cecum; rarely in rectum
NE-A 2 of 4
• PCC/PGL:
Clinical Signs or Symptoms revised: . . .sweating, paroxysmal headache, pallor episodes, syncope (very rare)
Testing, first bullet revised: Plasma free or 24-hour urine fractionated total metanephrines and normetanephrines catecholamines
• Pituitary tumor, Testing:
Fourth bullet revised: Alpha subunits (mainly in patients with non-secretory tumors)
Sixth bullet revised: Plasma adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)/random AM cortisol
• Hypercortisolemia, Clinical Signs or Symptoms revised: Cushing syndrome (central weight gain, striae, hypertension, hyperglycemia, depression, 
hirsutism, muscle weakness)
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors include:
Continued

Version 1.2026, 04/21/2026 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines® (Oncology Guidelines®), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
NE-B 1 of 5
• Page header revised: Principles of Imaging for Neuroendocrine Neoplasms
• Anatomic Imaging:
Second bullet revised: . . .When possible, SSTR-PET/CT and SSTR-PET/MRI should be performed concurrently with diagnostic multiphase CT or 
MRI. Consider same imaging modality and contrast agent (either multiphasic CT or MRI) for follow-up studies for consistent imaging interpretation.
Third bullet revised: Without a known tumor or specific clinical concern, imaging should include chest CT ± contrast per institutional standards and 
imaging of the brain is generally not required for well-differentiated NET.
• Functional Imaging:
First bullet, new sub-bullet added: For qualitative lesion assessment, Modified Krenning score can be used. Krenning score is based on the lesion with 
the highest SSTR uptake:
 ◊0, no uptake
 ◊1, very low uptake
 ◊2, uptake ≤ liver
 ◊3, uptake > liver
 ◊4, uptake > spleen
Last bullet revised: Combining FDG-PET and SSTR-PET, even for lower grade tumors, and a combined score of FDG-PET and SSTR-PET gives 
more prognostic value and, outperforms pathologic grading, and may identify more active disease.
NE-B 3 of 5
• New Principles of Imaging for Pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma added.
NE-B 4 of 5
• New Principles of Imaging for Adrenocortical Carcinoma added.
NE-B 5 of 5
• New reference 2 added: Chan DL, Hayes AR, Karfis I, et al. Dual [68Ga]DOTATATE and [18F]FDG PET/CT in patients with metastatic 
gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine neoplasms: a multicentre validation of the NETPET score. Br J Cancer 2023;128:549-555.
NE-C
• New Principles of Endoscopic Treatment of Neuroendocrine Tumors added.
NE-D 1 of 3
• New bullets added:
Second bullet: Gastric, duodenal, and rectal NETs may be endoscopically resectable, see NE-I.
Fourth bullet, second sub-bullet, new tertiary-bullet added: Grade 1 PanNETs <2 cm can be safely observed and is the recommended treatment 
course. Grade 2 PanNETs should be considered for surgical resection if patient factors allow.
Fifth bullet: After simple appendectomy, upon examination of the specimen, if >2 cm and positive lymph nodes in the specimen, recommendation is 
complete staging imaging and proceed to right hemicolectomy. Margin positivity can be considered a high-risk feature based on size and discussion 
with patient for complete right hemicolectomy.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
NE-D 1 of 3 (continued)
• Bullets revised:
Third bullet: Resection of GI NETs should include adequate regional lymph node resection (including all palpable disease where feasible). This is
especially important for small bowel NETs were manual palpation of the entire length of bowel is recommended as the rate of synchronous primary 
tumors is high (15%–30% incidence) Palpate the small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal valve. . .
Sixth bullet: . . .Tumors of the head are generally treated with pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure); tumors of the body and tail are treated 
with distal pancreatectomy and with or without splenectomy or spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. . .
NE-D 2 of 3
• Bullets revised:
Second bullet: Cytoreductive surgery of >90% of metastatic disease may provide symptomatic relief, prevent future symptoms, and improve 
progression-free survival for patients with functioning tumors. . .
Sixth bullet: Octreotide therapy should be considered parenterally prior to induction of anesthesia in patients with carcinoid syndrome to prevent
carcinoid crisis. Prophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis. Intraoperative octreotide administration should 
not delay vasopressor use and fluid administration to treat carcinoid crisis.
• New bullet added: For patients with clinical stage I or II ACC (or suspected ACC), achieving R0 resection is of critical importance. It is recommended 
that these operations be performed by surgeons with high volume experience in adrenal surgery. Although an open approach is typically advised, 
choosing a minimally invasive technique should rely on the surgeon’s experience.
NE-D 3 of 3
• New references added:
Reference 4: Mayo SC, de Jong MC, Pulitano C, et al. Surgical management of hepatic neuroendocrine tumor metastasis: Results from an 
international multi-institutional analysis. Ann Surg Oncol 2010;17:3129-3136.
Reference 5: Gudmundsdottir H, Habermann EB, Vierkant RA, et al. Survival and symptomatic relief after cytoreductive hepatectomy for 
neuroendocrine tumor liver metastases: Long-term follow-up evaluation of more than 500 patients. Ann Surg Oncol 2023;30:4840-4851.
Reference 6: Yip L, Duh QY, Wachtel H, et al. American Association of Endocrine Surgeons Guidelines for Adrenalectomy: Executive Summary. JAMA 
Surg 2022;157:870-877.
• Reference removed: Maxwell JE, Naraev B, Halperin DM, Choti MA, Halfdanarson TR. Shifting paradigms in the pathophysiology and treatment of 
carcinoid crisis. Ann Surg Oncol 2022;29:3072-3084.
NE-E 1 of 9
• Required Information, first bullet, third sub-bullet revised: In cases of mixed neuroendocrine neoplasms (with the smaller tumor component at least 30% 
of the total tumor mass), each component must be diagnosed and graded based on AJCC WHO tumor nomenclature and following the criteria of tumor 
differentiation.

Discussion
NE-J 1 of 3 (continued)
• Preparing Eligible Patients for Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate:
New bullets added:
 ◊Prior to PRRT, surgical evaluation should be performed. Resection of primary tumors may reduce the risk of PRRT-induced bowel obstruction or 
ischemia and decrease of overall tumor burden may improve PRRT responses. Additionally, liver cytoreduction post PRRT can be more technically 
challenging.
 ◊Short-term toxicities:
Third bullet revised: Counsel patients about the long-term risks of:
 ◊Second sub-bullet revised: Myelosuppression, less commonly secondary myelodysplastic syndrome and leukemia
 ◊Fourth sub-bullet revised: Hepatic toxicity (in patients with extremely high liver tumor burden or preexisting liver dysfunction)
 ◊New sub-bullet added: Bowel obstruction and bowel ischemia (rare) in the setting of disease in the root of mesentery occluding mesenteric vessels 
or peritoneal/mesenteric carcinomatosis. Consider post-treatment prophylactic steroids in patients at risk.
Bullet removed: Do not administer long-acting SSAs (eg, octreotide LAR, lanreotide) for 4 weeks prior to each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate treatment. 
Administer short-acting octreotide as needed for symptom control of carcinoid syndrome; discontinue at least 24 hours prior to initiating lutetium Lu 
177 dotatate.
NE-J 2 of 3
• Dose and Administration:
First bullet revised: Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate is administered via peripheral IV at a dose of 200 mCi over 30–40 minutes every 8 weeks for a total of 4 
treatments (unless dose modification required secondary to adverse reactions). Administration is by gravity drip or programmable infusion pump.
Second bullet, sub-bullets revised:
 ◊Second sub-bullet: In general, amino acids are administered 30 minutes before, concurrently with, and 3 hours after lutetium Lu 177 dotatate.
 ◊Third sub-bullet revised: Solutions containing only arginine/lysine are preferred as they only available through compounding pharmacies, but are 
much less emetogenic than commercial amino acid solutions, but these are only available through compounding pharmacies. Two Options options 
for renal protective compounded amino acids administration are as follows:
– First tertiary bullet revised: Arginine 2.5%/lysine 2.5% in 1000 mL NaCl infused at 250 mL/h for 4 hours. Routine prophylactic antiemetics may not 
be necessary if selective (arginine/lysine) amino acid solutions are used, but should be available as needed.
	
– Second tertiary bullet revised: Commercial amino acid formulation (nonselective, typically containing approximately 20 amino acids) mixed in 
sterile water for a total volume of approximately 2000 mL. Aggressive antiemetic prophylaxis is recommended for patients when nonselective 
amino acid solutions are used. Infusion rate of the amino acids can be increased to roughly 300–500 mL/h, as tolerated. Recommend starting 
at low rate of 50 mL/h and increasing by 10 mL/h every 10 minutes as tolerated based on symptoms such as nausea. Lutetium Lu 177 dotatate 
infusion should begin after at least 250 mL of amino acids have been infused.
 ◊Sub-bullet removed: Commercial (nonselective) amino acid formulations infused at high rates are more emetogenic than compounded amino acids.
New bullet added: Although acute hormonal reactions are rare, SSA should be readily available for urgent management depending on the syndrome 
or the symptoms.
Bullet removed: Antiemetic medications should be available prior to and during amino acid and lutetium Lu 177 dotatate infusions. Aggressive 
antiemetic prophylaxis is recommended for patients when nonselective amino acid solutions are used with PRRT. Routine prophylactic antiemetics 
may not be necessary if selective (arginine/lysine) amino acid solutions are used, but should be available as needed.

NE-J 2 of 3 (continued)
• Post-treatment Instructions:
Third bullet revised: SSAs (octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide) can be administered 4–24 hours after each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate PRRT 
treatment.
New bullet added: Starting within 24 hours of PRRT ending, consider prophylactic steroid taper. Regimens include methylprednisolone 6-day taper or 
21-day dexamethasone taper.
• Timing of SSAs (Octreotiede or Lanreotide) in Relation to Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate:
Third bullet revised: There are Due to theoretical concerns regarding the competition between SSAs and lutetium Lu 177 dotatate for SSTR binding, 
clinical trials that have used PRRT and FDA guidance require/recommend withholding SSAs for at least 4 weeks prior to each PRRT cycle, however 
there is little evidence to support this practice. The Panel notes that it does not need to be strictly 28 days. Therefore, the following actions are 
recommended:
◊Sub-bullets removed:
– Do not administer long-acting SSAs for 4 weeks prior to each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate treatment.
– Stop short-acting SSAs 24 hours before each lutetium LU 177 dotatate treatment.
– SSAs (short- and long-acting) can be resumed 4–24 hours after each lutetium Lu 177 dotatate treatment.
NE-J 3 of 3
• New references added:
Reference 11: Singh S, Halperin D, Myrehaug S, et al. [177Lu]Lu-DOTA-TATE plus long-acting octreotide versus high‑dose long-acting octreotide for 
the treatment of newly diagnosed, advanced grade 2–3, well-differentiated, gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumours (NETTER-2): An open-
label, randomised, phase 3 study. Lancet 2024;403:2807-2817.
Reference 13: Pelle E, Al-Toubah T, El-Haddad G, et al. Risk of bowel ischemia in patients with mesenteric neuroendocrine tumors after treatment 
with 177Lu-DOTATATE. Endocr Oncol 2025;5:e250033.
Reference 14: Strosberg JR, Al-Toubah T, Pellè E, et al. Risk of bowel obstruction in patients with mesenteric or peritoneal disease receiving peptide 
receptor radionuclide therapy. J Nucl Med 2021;62:69-72.
Reference 15: Bardasi C, Benatti S, Luppi G, et al. Carcinoid crisis: A misunderstood and unrecognized oncological emergency. Cancers (Basel) 
2022;14:662.
NE-K 1 of 3
• Carcinoid Syndrome, fifth bullet revised: Octreotide should be available as needed during surgical procedures for patients with carcinoid syndrome 
who develop hemodynamic instability that could indicate carcinoid crisis. Doses of octreotide (100–500 mcg IV) can be used, potentially followed by IV 
infusion of octreotide (50–300 mcg/h). Prophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis. Intraoperative octreotide 
administration should not delay vasopressor use and fluid administration to treat carcinoid crisis.
NE-K 3 of 3
• Insulin, Management, first bullet revised: Stabilize glucose levels with diet (complex carbohydrates) and/or diazoxide or everolimus.

NET-1A
a	Principles of Pathology (NE-E).
b The minimum standard is to obtain at least two biopsies from the antrum (lesser and greater curvature), one biopsy from the incisura (angularis), and two biopsies from 
the gastric body (lesser and greater curvature). Dixon MF, et al. Am J Surg Pathol 1996;20:1161-1181.
c Serum gastrin can be falsely elevated with proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use. To confirm diagnosis, it should ideally be checked when fasting and off PPI for >1 week. 
However, PPI should be continued in patients with overt clinical symptoms of gastrinoma and/or risks of complications.
d Elevated gastrin levels are usually suggestive of type 1 or type 2 tumors.
e For rare, >2 cm, type 1 gastric tumors, workup should include multiphasic CT or MRI of the abdomen. For metastatic disease, see NET-9.
f Type 3 gastric NETs (gNETs) are sporadic and unifocal.
g There is increasing evidence that patients who have been on long-term PPI therapy may be at an increased risk of developing gNETs, which appear to have a much 
lower propensity to metastasize than sporadic type 3 tumors.
h Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases.
i SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following 
administration of SSAs.
j SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC).
k Hirasawa T, et al. Dig Endosc 2021;33:408-417.
l Endoscopic resection should be reserved for small (<1 cm), superficial, low-grade tumors.

h Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases.
x Tumor/somatic molecular profiling should be considered for patients with locoregional 
unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify 
actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be 
considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible. 
gg For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg 
SC every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For breakthrough symptoms, 
octreotide 100–250 mcg SC TID can be considered. 
hh If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA.
kk If clinically significant disease progression, treatment with 
octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should be discontinued for 
non-functional tumors and continued in patients with functional 
tumors; these regimens may be used in combination with any of the 
subsequent options. 
ll For symptom control, consider addition of focal therapy (eg, 
endobronchial therapy, ablation).
pp NETs are highly heterogeneous and all elements need to be 
considered (eg, burden of disease, symptoms, histopathology, rate 
of growth) when determining the best course of treatment. 
qq Observation can be considered in select patients with low burden 
of disease and favorable prognostic features.

a Principles of Pathology (NE-E).
b Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases.
c SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. Data are limited on the optimal timing of scans following 
administration of SSAs.
d SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC).
e Observation can be considered for small (≤2 cm), low-grade, incidentally discovered, non-functional tumors. Decision based on estimated surgical risk, site of 
tumor, and patient comorbidities (Sadot E, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2016;23:1361-1370; Partelli S, et al. Br J Surg 2022;109:1186-1190; Heidsma CM, et al. Br J Surg 
2021;108:888-891). Follow surveillance recommendations on PanNET-11.

MANAGEMENT OF PRIMARY NON-METASTATIC DISEASE
e Observation can be considered for small (≤2 cm), low-grade, incidentally discovered, non-functional tumors. Decision based on estimated surgical risk, site of 
tumor, and patient comorbidities (Sadot E, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2016;23:1361-1370; Partelli S, et al. Br J Surg 2022;109:1186-1190; Heidsma CM, et al. Br J Surg 
2021;108:888-891). Follow surveillance recommendations on PanNET-11.
f Patients with germline mutations are treated based on any tumors of a concerning size.
g Consider belzutifan for resectable tumors in the setting of germline VHL alteration. The decision to use belzutifan in small resectable tumors needs to be individualized. 
Data for the use of belzutifan for larger tumors, locally advanced unresectable tumors, and distant disease are extremely limited. Clinical trials are ongoing.
h Preoperative vaccination against encapsulated bacterium (ie, pneumococcus, haemophilus influenzae b, meningococcal group C), if considering surgery with possible 
splenectomy. See Principles of Surgical Management of Neuroendocrine Tumors (NE-D 2 of 3).
i As appropriate, central pancreatectomy or spleen-preserving surgery can be considered. 
j PanNETs that are 1–2 cm have a small, but real risk of lymph node metastases. Therefore, lymph node resection should be considered.

bb If clinically significant disease progression, treatment with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should 
be discontinued for non-functional tumors and continued in patients with functional tumors; these 
regimens may be used in combination with any of the subsequent options. 
cc Staged or synchronous resection when possible. When performing staged pancreatoduodenectomy 
and liver resection, consider hepatectomy prior to pancreatic resection in order to reduce risk of 
perihepatic sepsis. De Jong MC, et al. Ann Surg 2010;252:142-148.
dd Use of belzutifan in the setting of germline VHL alteration needs to be individualized (category 2B). 
Data for the use of belzutifan for larger tumors, locally advanced unresectable tumors, and distant 
disease are extremely limited. Clinical trials are ongoing.
ee For patients with insulinoma, octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide should be used only if SSTR-based 
imaging is positive. If used, they should be used with caution in patients with insulinoma as they may 
transiently worsen hypoglycemia (see Discussion for details).
ff In select cases, it may be appropriate to proceed to front-line systemic therapy or locoregional therapy 
prior to or concurrently with octreotide acetate LAR or lanreotide.

a SSTR-negative refers to tumors that show insufficient uptake on SSTR-based imaging (lower than liver, Krenning score 0–2). 
b SSTR-negative disease and mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease is more common with higher grade (G3) disease and atypical lung carcinoid. 
c Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases.
d In select cases, it may be appropriate to proceed to front-line systemic therapy or locoregional therapy prior to or concurrently with octreotide acetate LAR or 
lanreotide.
e Observation is an option for SSTR-negative, non-functional tumors with low disease burden. Close follow-up (eg, restaging scans every 3–6 months) is recommended 
because SSTR-negative disease has a worse prognosis. Refardt J, et al. Endocr Relat Cancer 2020;27:615-624. 
f Consider use of SSAs in patients with SSTR-negative disease and non-functional tumors.
g Close follow-up (eg, restaging scans every 3–6 months) is recommended for patients with SSTR-negative disease who are treated with SSAs.
h PRRT can be considered for mixed SSTR-positive/negative disease but it might not work as well as for homogenous SSRT-positive disease. Treatment may also need 
to incorporate strategies to address the SSTR-negative disease. See Principles of Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy with Lutetium Lu 177 Dotatate (NE-J).
i For symptom and/or tumor control, octreotide acetate LAR 20–30 mg IM or lanreotide 120 mg SC every 4 weeks. Higher doses have been shown to be safe. For 
breakthrough symptoms, octreotide 100–250 mcg SC TID can be considered.
j If injection site-related complications occur, consider switching to another SSA.

identify actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible. 
g There are limitations in terms of the data for what the appropriate cutoff should be, as well as variability/heterogeneity of Ki-67 in a given tumor and over time in serial 
biopsies. The clinical course can be heterogeneous and treatment considerations need to account for both pathologic and clinical features.

e SSTR-based imaging (SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI) is not 
part of the routine evaluation of PDNECs.
f Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases.
g Tumor/somatic molecular profiling is recommended for patients with locoregional unresectable/
metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to identify actionable alterations. 
Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue 
testing is not feasible.
h Combination of immune checkpoint inhibitors + chemotherapy is investigational for all patients with 
extrapulmonary PDNECs. 
i Earlier, if symptoms.
j Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents

PHEO-1
EVALUATIONa,b
TUMOR TYPE
PCC/PGL
Recommended:
• Plasma free or 24-h urine fractionated total metanephrines and 
catecholaminesc,d,e (NE-A 2 of 4)
• Adrenal protocol CT or MRI if not already done (AGT-1)
• Genetic counseling and testing for inherited genetic syndromes (NE-F)
As appropriate, if metastatic or multifocal disease suspectedf (NE-B):
• Multiphasicg abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI
• SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRId,h,i
• FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh)
• Chest CT ± contrast
• Meta-iodobenzylguanidine (MIBG) scan with single-photon emission CT 
(SPECT)/CTj
• Consider molecular profiling of tumor tissuek
PHEO-2
a Principles of Pathology (NE-E).
b Consider medical alert ID for hormonally secreting PCCs and PGLs in situ or metastatic disease.
c Review concurrent medication(s) for those that may interfere with plasma metanephrines evaluation. Elevations that are 3 times above the upper limit of normal are 
diagnostic. 
d For cervical PGL, measuring serum and/or 24-hour urine fractionated catecholamines (for dopamine) or methoxytyramine (if available) may be appropriate. 
Methoxytyramine is helpful as the metabolite of dopamine in cervical tumors but it is not commercially available in many places.
e Both catecholamines and metanephrines/normetanephrines can represent false-positive results. Some investigators support the use of fractionated urinary and/or 
plasma catecholamines as a way to differentiate false-positive results and to differentiate epinephrine-secreting PCC. 
f Data on the role of functional imaging in PCC/PGL are evolving and the preferred method remains unclear.
g Multiphasic imaging studies are performed with IV contrast in arterial and portal venous phases.
h SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI of skull vertex to mid-thigh with multiphase IV contrast when possible. 
i SSTR-PET tracers (eg, 68Ga-DOTATATE, 64Cu-DOTATATE, and 68Ga-DOTATOC).
j MIBG scans are less sensitive than FDG-PET and 68Ga-DOTATATE for metastatic and multifocal PCCs/PGLs (in patients with von Hippel Lindau [VHL] and succinate 
dehydrogenase [SDH] syndromes but not patients with MEN1 or NF1 syndromes or some patients with sporadic PCC). SPECT/CT imaging of involved sites is 
recommended. Obtain MIBG scan if considering treatment with I131-MIBG. Timmers HJLM, et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 2012;104:700-708.
k Tumor/somatic molecular profiling should be considered for patients with locoregional unresectable/metastatic disease who are candidates for anticancer therapy to 
identify actionable alterations. Testing on tumor tissue is preferred; however, ctDNA testing can be considered if tumor tissue testing is not feasible.

MEDICAL PREPARATION FOR TREATMENTl
PHEO-2
SURVEILLANCE
b Consider medical alert ID for hormonally secreting 
PCCs and PGLs in situ or metastatic disease. 
l Avoid medications that will precipitate hormone-
mediated crisis.
m If the patient has a hypertensive crisis, consider 
addition of nitroprusside, phentolamine, or 
nicardipine for treatment of hypertension. Cardiac 
arrhythmias can be managed with esmolol or 
lidocaine. Hypoglycemia and hypotension can be 
seen postoperatively. Treat with IV fluids for volume 
expansion and include dextrose for glucose levels.
n Alpha 1 selective receptor blockers include terazosin, doxazosin, and prazosin, and non-selective receptor 
blockers include phenoxybenzamine. Therapy for 7–14 days is recommended prior to surgical therapy. 
Nonselective alpha blockade phentolamine (IV) can be used intraoperatively. Doxazosin has a longer half-life 
and is oftentimes more available than some of the other agents.
o Alpha blockade is necessary treatment for all hormonally secreting PCCs and PGLs regardless of clinical 
symptoms. If additional treatments are planned, patients may need medication adjustments. After alpha 
blockade, if additional blood pressure support is needed, the addition of dihydropyridine calcium channel 
blockers can be used. This is not recommended as monotherapy unless the patient cannot tolerate alpha 
blockade. Metyrosine can also be used in addition to alpha blockade to stabilize blood pressure. Beta blockade 
can be added to alpha blockade for tachycardia. B1 selective blockers or nonselective beta blockers can be 
used. Combination beta/alpha blockers are not recommended.
p Fishbein L, et al. Pancreas 2021;50:469-493.

o FDA-approved test recommended for determination of TMB.
p May require removal of adjacent structures (ie, liver, kidney, pancreas, spleen, diaphragm) for complete resection.
q It is important to achieve negative margins and avoid breaching the tumor capsule. There may be an increased risk for local recurrence and peritoneal spread when 
done with a minimally invasive approach. 
r If bulky disease, or <90% is removable, surgery can be reconsidered following response to systemic therapy.
s High-risk local recurrence features include: positive margins, Ki-67 >10%, rupture of capsule, large size, and high grade.
t Monitor Mitotane blood levels. Some institutions recommend target levels of 14–20 mcg/mL if tolerated. Steady-state levels may be reached several months after 
initiation of Mitotane. Life-long hydrocortisone ± fludrocortisone replacement usually is required with Mitotane.
u Mitotane may have more benefit for control of hormone symptoms than control of tumor.
v Optimal duration is unknown. The role of adjuvant chemotherapy is under investigation.

c Further studies should be done, especially in patients with suspected MEN1, when the serum calcium is ≥10 mg/dL since normocalcemic hyperparathyroidism is noted 
especially as an early sign of the disease. Hyperparathyroidism evaluations should include consideration of other secondary causes of hypercalcemia.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
• In general, surgical treatment of patients with MEN1 is similar to those with sporadic tumors. Refer to the relevant site-specific 
recommendations for PanNETs earlier in these guidelines (PanNET-1 through PanNET-10).
• However, one notable exception is the multifocality of pancreatoduodenal NETs in patients with MEN1. The role of surgery remains 
controversial in patients with multifocal tumors.
• Decision to resect a pancreatic or duodenal NET in the setting of multifocal disease is complex. If surgery is performed to resect hormonally 
functional tumor(s), attempts should be made to preoperatively localize the site of the functional tumor. Surgical resection can be 
considered in the following scenarios:
Symptomatic functional tumors refractory to medical management
Tumor >2 cm in size
Tumor with relatively rapid rate of growth over 6–12 months 
• MEN1-associated metastatic PanNETs are often slower growing than metastatic sporadic tumors. Observation can be considered for non-
functioning indolent tumors.
MEN1-3

Medullary thyroid carcinoma:

cervical lymph nodes
See Oncology Guidelines 
for Thyroid Carcinoma
a Preference of scan will depend on institutional practice/protocol. Sestamibi scan may not be as sensitive as other imaging options since often the patient has 
hyperplasia.
b Evaluation of PCC should be done before the administration of any anesthetic or invasive procedure.
c More likely to be multifocal.
d For synchronous bilateral PCCs, a bilateral adrenalectomy is recommended.
e Consider medical alert ID for hormonally secreting PCCs in situ or metastatic disease.
f Review concurrent medication(s) for those that may interfere with plasma metanephrines evaluation. Elevations that are 3 times above the upper limit of normal are 
diagnostic.
g Both catecholamines and metanephrines/normetanephrines can represent false-positive results. Some investigators support the use of fractionated urinary and/or 
plasma catecholamines as a way to differentiate false-positive results and to differentiate epinephrine-secreting PCC.
h For the treatment of synchronous tumors, surgical resection of PCC should take priority over thyroidectomy for medullary thyroid carcinoma.
i Subtotal parathyroidectomy is recommended when all the parathyroid glands are abnormal. Some thyroid surgeons recommend total parathyroidectomy with 
parathyroid autotransplantation, but others believe the risk of hypoparathyroidism (~6%) is too high to warrant this procedure.

header: PRINCIPLES OF BIOCHEMICAL TESTING1-9
content:
• Some NETs can secrete specific neuroendocrine hormones. Hormonal workup should be guided by the presence of symptoms of the excess hormone. Screening for 
hormones in individuals with asymptomatic disease is not routinely required.
Patients with functional tumors have clinical symptoms related to tumor-associated hormone excess.
• PPIs, other drugs, some medical conditions, and certain foods are known to cause false elevations in serum gastrin and chromogranin A. To confirm diagnosis, serum 
gastrin should ideally be checked when fasting and off PPI for >1 week. However, PPI should be continued in patients with overt clinical symptoms of gastrinoma and/
or risks of complications.
• If MEN2 is suspected, patients should be evaluated for PCC/PGL prior to any procedures.8

header: PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING FOR NEUROENDOCRINE NEOPLASMS
content:
NE-B
1 OF 5
Anatomic Imaging
• Cross-sectional imaging should include the primary site of disease. Either CT or MRI is appropriate.
• The liver is a common metastatic site for NET, and these metastatic lesions are often hypervascular. Multiphase (dual-phase) contrast-enhanced 
(arterial and portal venous phase) CT is important because NETs frequently enhance in the arterial phase. SSTR-PET/CT should be interpreted 
in combination with dual-phase CT or MRI to identify both SSTR(+) and SSTR(-) sites of disease. PET/CT and PET/MRI scanners are capable of 
providing full diagnostic multiphase contrast-enhanced CT and MRI scans. This provides the most accurate correlation of anatomic sites and receptor 
status. When possible, SSTR-PET/CT and SSTR-PET/MRI should be performed concurrently with diagnostic multiphase CT or MRI. Consider same 
imaging modality and contrast agent (either multiphasic CT or MRI) for follow-up studies for consistent imaging interpretation.
• Without a known tumor or specific clinical concern, imaging should include chest CT ± contrast per institutional standards and imaging of the brain is 
generally not required for well-differentiated NET.
• For metastatic well-differentiated NET, anatomic imaging should generally be performed every 12 weeks–12 months based on clinical or pathologic 
signs of aggressiveness.
• Consider MRI over CT to minimize radiation risk.
Functional Imaginga
• Evaluation with SSTR imaging to assess receptor status and distant disease is appropriate. This is especially important for determining whether a 
patient may benefit from SSTR-directed therapy.  
SSTR-based imaging options include SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI, or octreotide SPECT/CT (only if SSTR-PET is not available).
Examples of appropriate SSTR-PET tracers: 68Ga-DOTATATE, 68Ga-DOTATOC, or 64Cu-DOTATATE.
SSTR-positive if uptake in measurable lesions is greater than liver.
For qualitative lesion assessment, Modified Krenning score can be used. Krenning score is based on the lesion with the highest SSTR uptake: 
 ◊0, no uptake 
 ◊1, very low uptake 
 ◊2, uptake ≤ liver 
 ◊3, uptake > liver 
 ◊4, uptake > spleen
• Whenever possible, SSTR-PET/CT should be performed in combination with contrast-enhanced CT or MRI (dual-phase hepatic CT or MRI imaging 
preferred) to minimize the total number of imaging studies. The contrast-enhanced CT or MRI is vital to identify lesions that are SSTR-negative as well 
as those that are SSTR-positive. 
• Octreotide SPECT/CT is much less sensitive for defining SSTR-positive disease than SSTR-PET/CT, and typically cannot be done in combination with 
multiphase CT or MRI. Therefore, SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI is preferred. 
• FDG-PET should be considered in select cases where G2 or higher NETs or NECs is documented.
• As with SSTR-PET/CT, combining FDG-PET with dual-phase liver CT or MRI is preferred.
• Combining FDG-PET and SSTR-PET, even for lower grade tumors, and a combined score of FDG-PET and SSTR-PET gives more prognostic value, 
outperforms pathologic grading, and may identify more active disease.1,2
a Data on the role of functional imaging in PCC/PGL is evolving and the preferred method remains unclear.
References

Table of Contents
Discussion
Surveillance
• After potentially curative surgery, surveillance is recommended for at least 10 years for most patients. In certain cases, surveillance may be extended 
beyond 10 years based on risk factors such as age and risk of recurrence. However, data are limited on the optimal surveillance schedule beyond 10 
years.
Transthoracic Echocardiogram to Assess for Carcinoid (NET-Related) Heart Disease3,4
• Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) is important for the evaluation of CHD and should include morphologic evaluation of the valves (especially 
tricuspid and pulmonary), as well as assessment of the right heart size and function. When valve disease is present, an agitated saline injection should 
be performed to determine whether there is an atrial level shunt. 
• Post valve replacement, patients should be followed with serial echocardiograms according to institutional practice. If valves are abnormal by 
echocardiogram, consider the potential for clots requiring resumption of anticoagulants versus recurrent CHD.
NE-B
2 OF 5
Repeat TTE Recommendations for Patients with NETs:
Without known CHD
• Symptoms of dyspnea, fatigue, edema, or ascites
• Physical exam findings including elevated jugular venous pressure, edema, or ascites
• Prior to planned bowel/liver resection
• Reassessment every 1–3 years
Known CHD with or 
without prior valve 
surgery
• Decision-making in conjunction with a multidisciplinary care team
• Annual reassessment of all valves (native ± prosthetic) in conjunction with specialty cardiology consultation
• New symptoms of dyspnea, fatigue, edema, or ascites
• Change in physical exam including elevated jugular venous pressure, edema, or ascites
• Prior to planned bowel/liver resection

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Anatomic Imaging
• Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis should be considered as the first step in patients with biochemically confirmed PCC/PGL. If 
this initial imaging is negative, CT or MRI of the chest and dedicated MRI of the neck should be considered. 
• Consider MRI over CT to minimize radiation risk, especially in children.
• Depending upon the genetic mutations, being symptomatic and non-symptomatic, follow up with contrast-enhanced MRI of neck, thorax, abdomen, and 
pelvis is recommended.
Functional Imaging
• SSTR imaging, FDG PET, as well as iodine-123 metaiodobenzylguanidine (I-123 MIBG) scintigraphy.
• Evaluation with SSTR imaging to assess receptor status and distant disease is appropriate. SSTR imaging is particularly sensitive in patients with 
metastatic disease, with SDHx pathogenic variants, as well as with head and neck PCC/PGL.
• SSTR-based imaging options include SSTR-PET/CT or SSTR-PET/MRI, or examples of appropriate SSTR-PET tracers: 68Ga-DOTATATE, 68Ga-
DOTATOC, or 64Cu-DOTATATE.
• SSTR-positive if uptake in measurable lesions > liver.
• Whenever possible, SSTR-PET should be performed in combination with contrast-enhanced CT or MRI to minimize the total number of imaging 
studies. The contrast-enhanced CT or MRI is vital to identify lesions that are SSTR-negative as well as those that are SSTR-positive.
• FDG-PET may be considered if SSTR imaging is not available. Relatively decreased sensitivity due to brown fat activation, nonspecific uptake in 
inflammation, and uptake in other tumors. However, good sensitivity in PCC/PGL, particularly in patients harbouring germline pathogenic variants in 
cluster 1 genes, and in patients with metastatic PCC/PGL.
• 123I MIBG has lower sensitivity as a staging test in patients with cluster 1 PCC/PGL compared with patients with cluster 2 or sporadic PCC/PGL. 
Considering that most children with PCC/PGL harbour cluster 1 gene variants, I-123 MIBG should be typically reserved for patients with metastatic 
disease in whom radiolabelled iodine-131 metaiodobenzylguanidine (I-131 MIBG) therapy is being considered.

PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING FOR 
ADRENOCORTICAL CARCINOMA6-8 
Anatomic Imaging
• Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the abdomen are the standard imaging modalities for diagnosis, staging and restaging of the ACC. 
• For metastatic evaluation, consider contrast-enhanced CT chest, contrast-enhanced MRI brain.
Functional Imaging
• FDG-PET is an adjunct modality for local staging, recurrence, evaluation for metastatic disease, and treatment response.
• Bone scintigraphy may be considered for evaluation of metastatic bone disease.

Discussion
Gastric NETs (gNETs) (NE-C 1 of 6)
• Type 1 gNETs (NE-C 1 of 6)
• Type 2 gNETs (NE-C 2 of 6)
• Type 3 gNETs (NE-C 2 of 6)
• PPI-induced gNETs (NE-C 2 of 6)
Duodenal NETs (dNETs) (NE-C 3 of 6)
Rectal NETs (NE-C 4 of 6)
General
• For all gastric, duodenal, and rectal NETs, perform a high-definition white light endoscopy examination and evaluation using video chromoendoscopy 
and biopsy to determine grade and proliferative activity (Ki-67).
NE-C

header: PRINCIPLES OF ENDOSCOPIC TREATMENT OF NEUROENDOCRINE TUMORS

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
NE-C
1 OF 6
Type 1 gNETs1,2
• For lesions ≤1 cm3,4: 
G1/2; surveillance or endoscopic resection should be 
considered.3,5,6 
 ◊Polyectomy or EMR can be used for mucosal lesions.
Follow-up after endoscopic resection (polypectomy or EMR):
 ◊Clearance of larger/dominant lesions: consider 
endoscopic follow-up every 12–24 months.
– After several surveillance endoscopies, can consider 
increasing the interval between studies.
	
– Cross-sectional imaging is not necessary.
– Follow-up can primarily be managed by a 
gastroenterologist once the patient has seen an 
oncologist at least once.
 ◊Residual lesions: consider repeat endoscopy in 6 months 
to assess for residual disease.
– If residual disease is identified, consider endoscopic 
resection as outlined in these guidelines based upon 
size.2,7
	
– If no residual disease is identified, recommend an 
endoscopy in 12 months.
G3 (see WDG3-1); refer for surgical resection. Placement of 
endoscopic clip, ink, or tattoo is advisable to assist surgeon 
in tumor localization.
a	The minimum standard is to obtain at least two biopsies from the antrum (lesser and greater curvature), one biopsy from the incisura (angularis) and two biopsies from 
the gastric body (lesser and greater curvature).10
References
• For lesions >1 but ≤2 cm:
Consider EUS to determine the depth of invasion and assess for lymphadenopathy.
Consider cross-sectional imaging as clinically indicated.
G1/2, not involving the muscle layer; surveillance or endoscopic resection should 
be considered.3,5,6
 ◊EMR can be used for mucosal lesions.
 ◊ESD or eFTR should be considered for lesions involving the submucosa.5,7,8
Follow-up after endoscopic resection (polypectomy, EMR, ESD, or eFTR):
 ◊R0 resection: consider endoscopic follow-up every 12–24 months.
 ◊R1 resection: consider repeat endoscopy in 6 months to assess for residual 
disease.
– If residual disease is identified, consider EMR, ESD, or eFTR pending local 
expertise, if evidence of submucosal or deep invasion.2,7
	
– If no residual disease is identified, recommend an endoscopy in 12 months.7
 ◊Involving the muscle layer (T2) or concerning lymph nodes by EUS: recommend 
a multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.2,9
G3 (see WDG3-1), refer for surgical resection.
• For lesions >2 cm: 
EUS should be used to determine the depth of invasion and assess for 
lymphadenopathy. 
Cross-sectional imaging recommended.
Recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.2,9
gNETs
• It is important to determine the subtype using the following testing:
Perform gastrin lab test, ideally AM fasting, off PPI therapy for at least 1 week.
Perform gastric pH testing at the time of endoscopy.
Perform random mapping biopsies to rule out atrophic gastropathy, Helicobacter pylori, intestinal metaplasia, and determinate extent of disease.a If 
the subtype is not apparent, recommend multidisciplinary discussion.
• EUS should be used to determine the depth of invasion for all gNETs with the following characteristics:
Size requirements based upon subtype (see other sections)
Or ulceration
Or Grade 3
Or positive LVI during initial sampling

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
NE-C
2 OF 6
References
Type 2 gNETs
• Recommend cross-sectional imaging and PET-dotatate imaging.
• Recommend multidisciplinary approach to treat the underlying gastrinoma.
Type 3 gNETs
• EUS and CT should be used to determine the depth of invasion and assess for lymphadenopathy.
• Recommend cross-sectional imaging and consider PET-dotatate imaging.
• Recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
• Staging evaluation is recommended based on present guidelines to evaluate for distant metastasis.
• For lesions ≤1.5 cm, G1, not involving the muscle layer, and no concerning lymph nodes; endoscopic resection may be considered.11,12
EMR can be used for mucosal lesions.
ESD or eFTR should be considered for lesions involving the submucosa.
Follow-up after endoscopic resection (polypectomy, EMR, ESD, or eFTR):
 ◊R0 resection: endoscopic surveillance is not well established, however, consider endoscopic follow-up every 12 months.
 ◊R1 resection: consider surgical evaluation if repeat endoscopic resection is not an option.12,13
• Lesions >1.5 cm, G2/3, involving the muscle layer or concerning lymph nodes; recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
PPI-induced gNETs
• There is increasing evidence that patients who have been on long-term PPI therapy may be at an increased risk of developing gNETs, which appear to 
have a much lower propensity to metastasize than sporadic type 3 tumors.14-19 
• Data on endoscopic approach is limited at this time, however, would likely parallel the approach for type 1 gNETs given preliminary studies on the 
behavior of this group of gNETs.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
NE-C
3 OF 6
Duodenal NETs (dNETs)20,21
• EUS should be used to determine the depth of invasion for all dNETs with the following characteristics:
Periampullary involvement
Size >1 cm
Tumors extending beyond the submucosa on initial sampling or EUS
Functional dNETs
Or ulceration
Or Grade 3
Or positive LVI during initial sampling
• For periampullary dNETs, recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.22,23
• Non-periampullary dNETs:
Tumors ≤1 cm24-26:
 ◊G1/2, and negative LVI; endoscopic resection should be considered.
Tumors <0.5 cm, polypectomy or endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) may be attempted. 
Tumors 0.5–1 cm; EMR, endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD), or endoscopic full-thickness resection (eFTR) may be considered pending local 
expertise.
Follow-up after endoscopic resection (polypectomy, EMR, ESD, or eFTR):
 ◊R0 resection: endoscopic surveillance is not well established; however, consider follow-up endoscopy annually for 5 years.23,27
 ◊R1 resection: consider surgical evaluation if repeat endoscopic resection, using size-dependent approaches above, is not an option.
Tumors >1 cm, G3, positive LVI, or NEC; recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
References

4 OF 6

header: PRINCIPLES OF ENDOSCOPIC TREATMENT OF NEUROENDOCRINE TUMORS
content:
References
Rectal NETs
• For tumors ≤1 cm28-32: 
Tumors ≥0.5 cm, consider EUS and/or MRI.33
G1/Low G2, and negative LVI; consider endoscopic resection or transanal surgical excision, pending local expertise.
High G2c/3, positive LVI or ≥T2; recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
Follow-up after initial endoscopic resection (polypectomy, EMR, ESD, eFTR, or transanal surgical excision):
 ◊R0 resection:
– G1/Low G2 and negative LVI: follow-up should be individualized; however endoscopic surveillance may follow standard colorectal cancer 
screening guidelines.34
	
– High G2c/3 or positive LVI, or ≥T2: further endoscopic removal should not be attempted.
 ◊R1 resection35:
– G1/Low G2 and negative LVI: consider repeat endoscopy and biopsy.
▪If residual disease present, consider endoscopic resection or transanal surgical resection.36
	▪If no residual disease present, consider surveillance endoscopy in 12 months.
	
– High G2c/3 or positive LVI, or ≥T2: recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
• For tumors >1 cm to <2 cm37:
Consider EUS and/or MRI. 
Staging cross-sectional imaging and PET-dotatate is recommended. 
G1/Low G2; consider EMR, ESD, eFTR, or transanal surgical excision, pending local expertise.
High G2c/3, positive LVI, or ≥T2; recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
Follow-up after initial endoscopic resection (polypectomy, EMR, ESD, eFTR, or transanal surgical excision):
 ◊R0 resection: endoscopic follow-up should be individualized, however consider annual endoscopies for 5 years.30,38
 ◊R1 resection:39
– G1/Low G2, and negative LVI: consider repeat endoscopy and biopsy.
▪If residual disease present, consider ESD, eFTR or surgical resection.39 
	▪If no residual disease present, consider surveillance endoscopy in 12 months.
	
– High G2c/G3: multidisciplinary review is recommended along with surgical evaluation.
• Tumors ≥2 cm, recommend multidisciplinary discussion, including surgical evaluation.
EUS and/or MRI is recommended.
Staging cross-sectional imaging and PET-dotatate is recommended.
c	High G2 is defined as Ki-67 >10 %.40

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF NEUROENDOCRINE TUMORS
content:
• Surgical resection of lung tumors is recommended for localized and resectable locoregional disease. There are no randomized data regarding extent of 
surgical resection but retrospective analyses suggest that if the tumor can be resected completely, a sublobar resection may yield outcomes similar to a 
lobectomy even for atypical carcinoids.1
• Gastric, duodenal, and rectal NETs may be endoscopically resectable, see NE-I. 
• Resection of GI NETs should include adequate regional lymph node resection (including all palpable disease where feasible). Palpate the small bowel 
from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal valve. Resection of palpated lesions can be performed using a hybrid minimally invasive approach, if 
feasible.
• Standard oncologic surgery (eg, distal pancreatectomy/splenectomy or pancreatoduodenectomy) is appropriate for most resectable, non-metastatic 
PanNETs. However, there are additional considerations for the following circumstances:
Tumor enucleation should be considered primarily for insulinomas, which are highly symptomatic (hypoglycemic) but rarely malignant. Peripheral 
insulinomas should be considered for enucleation/local resection or spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy, when technically feasible.
For patients with small (<2 cm) low-grade PanNETs, decisions on surgery versus active surveillance need to be individualized, based on tumor size/
characteristics and patient characteristics:
 ◊Tumors <1 cm have a lower malignant potential than tumors measuring 1–2 cm.
 ◊Grade 1 PanNETs <2 cm can be safely observed and is the recommended treatment course. Grade 2 PanNETs should be considered for surgical 
resection if patient factors allow.
 ◊Other radiographic characteristics of small tumors (homogeneous, well-circumscribed, cystic) may also correlate with benign behavior.
 ◊Patient characteristics such as age and comorbidities are important when determining whether surveillance is appropriate.
 ◊Black patients with 1–2 cm low-grade PanNETs may have similar risk for lymph node metastases as white patients with >2 cm tumors.2 
 ◊Calcification is associated with higher tumor grade and increased rate of lymph node metastasis.3
• After simple appendectomy, upon examination of the specimen, if >2 cm and positive lymph nodes in the specimen, recommendation is complete 
staging imaging and proceed to right hemicolectomy. Margin positivity can be considered a high-risk feature based on size and discussion with patient 
for complete right hemicolectomy.
• Resection for larger (>2 cm) or malignant-appearing non-functional and functional PanNETs (ie, glucagonoma, VIPoma, somatostatinoma) should 
include total removal of the tumor with negative margins (including adjacent organs) and regional lymph nodes. Tumors of the head are generally 
treated with pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure); tumors of the body and tail are treated with distal pancreatectomy with or without 
splenectomy or spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. Generally surgery will include splenectomy, but with benign insulinoma, spleen preservation 
should be considered.
Assess implications of pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) in metastatic disease. In this setting, such surgery is usually not curative and 
impacts liver-directed therapy long-term.
• Resection of recurrent locoregional disease, isolated distant metastases, or a previously unresectable tumor that has regressed should be considered 
for selected patients with adequate performance status.
NE-D
1 OF 3
References

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
• In the setting of metastatic disease, resection of ileum/jejunum NETs (primary tumors and mesenteric lymph nodes) should be performed when 
symptoms arise from the primary tumor. In asymptomatic cases, resection should be considered to reduce future obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, 
bleeding, or perforation.
• Cytoreductive surgery of >90% of metastatic disease may provide symptomatic relief, prevent future symptoms, and improve progression-free survival.4,5 
This strategy is particularly appropriate for patients with relatively indolent metastatic small bowel NETs, and less appropriate for patients in whom rapid 
progression of disease is expected after surgery. Patients who are symptomatic from hormonal syndromes, such as carcinoid syndrome, typically derive 
palliation from cytoreductive surgery.
Parenchymal sparing surgery, including enucleation of liver metastases ± ablation, is preferred to preserve as much liver parenchyma as possible.
• Cholecystectomy is recommended when performing surgery for advanced NETs in patients anticipated to receive long-term somatostatin analogs 
(SSAs), as these patients are at higher risk of developing biliary symptoms and cholecystitis.
• Liver-directed therapies (eg, liver resection, thermal ablation, chemoembolization) for hepatic metastases from NETs following pancreatoduodenectomy 
are associated with increased risk for cholangitis and liver abscess.
• In cases of significant CHD, valve replacement should be done prior to liver resection if at all possible.
• Prophylactic octreotide is likely ineffective in preventing intraoperative carcinoid crisis. Intraoperative octreotide administration should not delay 
vasopressor use and fluid administration to treat carcinoid crisis.
• All patients who undergo a splenectomy should receive vaccination against encapsulated bacterium (ie, pneumococcus, haemophilus influenzae b, 
meningococcal group C). These vaccinations should be administered at least 14 days before elective splenectomy, if possible. If the vaccinations are 
not administered before surgery, they should be administered after the procedure as soon as the patient's condition is stable.
• For MEN1-related surgical principles, see MEN1-A.
• For MEN2-associated PCCs, subtotal adrenalectomy may be an option for select patients.
• For patients with clinical stage I or II ACC (or suspected ACC), achieving R0 resection is of critical importance. It is recommended that these operations 
be performed by surgeons with high volume experience in adrenal surgery. Although an open approach is typically advised, choosing a minimally 
invasive technique should rely on the surgeon’s experience.6

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGY FOR THE DIAGNOSIS OF NEUROENDOCRINE NEOPLASMS1
content:
Required Information:
• Diagnosis and differentiation:
Broad-spectrum keratin marker (to distinguish PCC/PGL)
2 of 3 immunohistochemistry (IHC) markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and INSM1)
In cases of mixed neuroendocrine neoplasms (with the smaller tumor component at least 30% of the total tumor mass), each component must be 
diagnosed and graded based on WHO tumor nomenclature and following the criteria of tumor differentiation.
• Grade (for well-differentiated NETs):
Ki-67 proliferative index (preferred, unless there is insufficient tissue) and/or mitotic rate
• Site of origin:
Clinical (including radiologic/endoscopic) information is used to determine if it is primary or metastatic, and if metastatic, the possible site of primary. 
• For resected specimens (surgical/endoscopic):
Tumor foci (unifocal/presence of multicentric disease)
Tumor size and extent (tumor node metastasis [TNM] stage per the AJCC TNM system [ST-1])
Presence of lymphovascular invasion (LVI)
Presence of perineural invasion
Margin status (report as positive or negative)
Lymph node status (including presence and size of mesenteric masses in gross report for jejunal and ileal NETs) (ST-1)
 ◊Include the number of positive nodes and total number of nodes examined
• Background mucosal pathology for clinicopathologic subtyping of gastric NETs (gNETs):
Secondary: Arising in gastric body mucosa linked to an underlying etiology:
 ◊Type 1: Hypergastrinemia with histologic evidence of background atrophy (either autoimmune or chronic H. pylori infection) associated with 
enterochromaffin-like (ECL)-cell hyperplasia.
 ◊Type 2: Hypergastrinemia from underlying gastrinoma with histologic evidence of parietal cell hyperplasia and ECL-cell hyperplasia.
 ◊Other: Hypergastrinemia (other than type 2) without evidence of underlying gastrinoma but with histologic evidence of parietal cell hyperplasia and 
ECL-cell hyperplasia, consider PPI-associated gNET.2 Other rare causes include insufficient proton pump activity due to underlying mutation.3
Primary: No underlying etiology identified and arising in normal background mucosa (either from gastric body or antrum):
 ◊Type 3: sporadic.
• Presence of other pathologic components (eg, non-neuroendocrine components)
Optional Information:
• IHC staining4,5
Site-specific markers (eg, TTF-1, CDX2, SATB2) can be used as indicated in well-differentiated NET.6
In challenging cases of high-grade neuroendocrine neoplasm, especially for pancreatic neuroendocrine neoplasm, Rb, p53,7 p16, ATRX, and DAXX 
may be used as a panel to help distinguish grade 3 NETs from NECs.a
If there is a clinical suspicion of Merkel cell carcinoma, CK20 and Merkel cell polyomavirus stains may be used.
Additional stains (eg, trypsin, chymotrypsin, BCL10) may be used in higher grade pancreatic neoplasms to exclude acinar cell carcinoma expressing 
neuroendocrine markers.
Hormone staining with IHC is not helpful and, if positive, it does not mean there is a functional tumor.
• Additional workup may be used as indicated if the morphology or staining characteristics are unusual.
NE-E 
1 OF 9
References
a Clinical, functional imaging, and molecular features should be taken into account as well.

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGY FOR THE DIAGNOSIS OF NEUROENDOCRINE NEOPLASMS1
content:
Functional Status
• Functioning NETs should have the same pathologic diagnosis as the non-functioning NETs at the same anatomic site, since the functional status is 
based upon clinical symptoms and should not alter the pathologic diagnosis.
Classification and Grade8
• Many classification schemes have been proposed for NETs.9-16 The most recent WHO classification system is suggested for gastroenteropancreatic 
(GEP) NETs and represents an attempt to unify European and American approaches.15 Multiple site-specific grading systems also exist.
Ki-67 Index:
 ◊Follow College of American Pathologists guidelines for assessment and specify exact Ki-67 proliferative index in report, not a range.
– Ki-67 index is reported as the percentage of positive tumor cells in the area of highest nuclear labeling (referred to as hot spots).
– Ki-67 should be performed on all types of specimens, primary or metastatic, biopsy or resection (as long as there are at least 500 tumor cells 
present and the specimen is assessable without crush artifact or significant intermixed inflammatory cells).
	
– Ki-67 proliferative index should also be assessed for every specimen sampled metachronously. For multiple sections sampled from a single 
tumor, Ki-67 stain should be performed on the block(s) of tumor with the highest mitotic index. Multiple tumor blocks may be used for this 
staining, and the tumor grade would be based on the highest proliferative index (specific example: Ki-67 proliferative index assessed for every 
focus of multifocal ileal NET as well as from mesenteric mass). When there are synchronous specimens from multiple primary tumors(s) and/or 
metastases, Ki-67 should be performed on at least 1 block from each of the primary and metastatic locations.
Mitotic Rate:
 ◊Mitotic rate should be based on counting mitoses in the areas of highest mitotic density, and should be reported as the number of mitoses per 2 
mm2.
 ◊Note that in cases where an accurate mitotic rate is precluded by inadequate tissue, such as in small biopsy samples including fine-needle 
aspiration, the Ki-67 index is the preferred method of establishing the proliferative rate.
• If both mitotic rate and Ki-67 index are used and these are discordant, it is currently recommended that the higher grade be used to assign 
classification.17
• The pathologist should report the actual parameters used to assign grade (ie, mitotic rate, proliferation index) so clinicians have the necessary 
information to make informed treatment decisions.
• Specific classification and grading scheme being utilized should be reported in parentheses after the diagnosis to avoid confusion with overlapping 
terminology and criteria used in other systems. 
• The raw data used to derive the grade should be reported. 
• Regardless of the system used, it is most important to realize that the term “neuroendocrine tumor” or “neuroendocrine carcinoma” without any further 
qualification as to grade is inadequate for prognostication and therapy and is inappropriate for pathology reporting.6,18

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Special Considerations:
• Keratin and neuroendocrine markers can be positive in certain neoplasms that may morphologically resemble, but are not classic neuroendocrine 
neoplasms. They are treated differently; therefore, correct identification or exclusion of these neoplasms in the appropriate clinical context is necessary. 
These include (but are not limited to) pancreatic acinar cell carcinomas, Merkel cell carcinomas, Ewing sarcoma, and desmoplastic round cell tumor. 
The terminology “carcinoma with neuroendocrine features” or “adenocarcinoma with neuroendocrine features” should be avoided, and a definite WHO-
recommended tumor classification should be attempted (adenocarcinomas in the GI tract can often express neuroendocrine markers with a patchy 
pattern of expression, without any survival or management significance).19 In case of ambiguous histology and challenges in interpreting the neoplasm, 
this should be stated clearly in the report, indicating the importance of clinical correlation, and recommending molecular testing (broad next-generation 
sequencing [NGS] panel).

classification of tumours series, 5th ed.; vol. 1). Available from: https://tumourclassification.iarc.who.int/chapters/31.
b Mitotic rates are to be expressed as the number of mitoses/2 mm2 (equaling 10 high-power fields at 40x magnification and an ocular field diameter of 0.5 mm) as 
determined by counting in 50 fields of 0.2 mm2 (ie, in a total area of 10 mm2); the Ki-67 proliferation index value is determined by counting at least 500 cells in the 
regions of highest labeling (hot spots), which are identified at scanning magnification; the final grade is based on whichever of the two proliferation indexes places the 
neoplasm in the higher grade category.
c PDNECs are not formally graded but are considered high grade by definition.
d In most MiNENs, both the neuroendocrine and non-neuroendocrine components are poorly differentiated, and the neuroendocrine component has proliferation indexes 
in the same range as other NECs, but this conceptual category allows for the possibility that one or both components may be well differentiated; when feasible, each 
component should therefore be graded separately.
